beats.narration_text: A photon passes an excited atom and keeps going — then two identical photons emerge, same direction, same phase.

card.headline: Why One Photon Can Become Two Identical Twins

beats.narration_text: Atoms have two energy levels. A photon of exactly the right energy can be absorbed, lifting the atom up. Later, the atom falls and emits a photon — but in a random direction. This is spontaneous emission.

Classical theory predicts only this: absorb and spontaneously re-emit. A brighter input means more emissions, but each photon fires randomly. No coherence, no directionality. Why does a laser beam exist at all?

card.headline: Absorption predicts random re-emission. A laser beam is not random. Why?

beats.narration_text: Einstein in 1917 found a third process hidden in the thermodynamics. When a photon passes an already-excited atom, it can trigger the atom to emit a second photon — coherent with the first: same direction, same phase, same frequency.

card.headline: A passing photon triggers an exact copy of itself.

beats.narration_text: The two photons are not just similar — they are quantum-coherent. They carry the same phase relationship. Each copy can trigger more copies. If excited atoms outnumber ground-state atoms — population inversion — the signal multiplies.

Population inversion requires pumping — driving more atoms up than down. In thermal equilibrium the lower level is always more populated. Inversion is non-equilibrium, maintained by continuous energy input.
Once inversion exists, each photon spawns copies faster than they are absorbed. The light avalanches. Theodore Maiman demonstrated this in 1960 in ruby — the first laser.

props.headline: Once inversion exists, each photon spawns copies faster than they are…

beats.narration_text: Stimulated photons are coherent: they interfere constructively and build into a single directional beam. Spontaneous photons scatter in all directions and wash out. The laser beam is the coherent avalanche, not the random drizzle.
Coherence is the signal; spontaneity is the noise.

card.headline: Stimulated copies add. Spontaneous emissions scatter.
One seed photon, three atoms, one chain

beats.narration_text: Illustrative numbers: one seed photon enters a medium with three excited atoms. It triggers atom 1 — two photons. Those two trigger atoms 2 and 3 — four photons. Four coherent photons emerge where one entered, all pointing the same direction. Double the atoms, double the output per pass. This is gain.

Why does one photon become two? Because quantum mechanics allows an excited atom to be triggered by a passing photon — and the triggered emission is a perfect quantum copy. That copy-on-demand is the laser principle. QUANTUM MECHANICS.

card.headline: A passing photon triggers an exact coherent copy — that is why a laser exists.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
